title: Apply: Second Amendment
description: Apply your knowledge of the Second Amendment to this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Second Amendment: Modern Cases and Debates

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1768 article in the *Boston Journal of the Times.***

Instances of the [[☃ definition 1]] and outrageous behavior of the military [[☃ definition 2]] still multiply upon us, some of which are of such nature, and have been carried to such lengths, as must serve fully to [[☃ definition 3]] that a [[☃ definition 4]] vote of this town, calling upon its inhabitants to provide themselves with arms for their defense, was a measure as [[☃ definition 5]] as it was legal. . . . It is a natural right which the people have reserved to themselves, confirmed by the [English] Bill of Rights to keep arms for their own defense; and as Mr. Blackstone observes: it is to be made use of when the sanctions of society and law are found insufficient to restrain the violence of oppression.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: officers in charge of keeping the peace

choices.content: The right to own a weapon for self-defense is a right for Englishmen, but not for colonists.
The misconduct of the military justifies the people embracing their right to arm themselves in self-defense.
The increasing military presence in the colonies has made it so that people no longer need to arm themselves.
The military has been violating people’s natural rights by confiscating their weapons from their homes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1768 article in the *Boston Journal of the Times.***

Instances of the [[☃ definition 2]] and outrageous behavior of the military [[☃ definition 3]] still multiply upon us, some of which are of such nature, and have been carried to such lengths, as must serve fully to [[☃ definition 4]] that a [[☃ definition 5]] vote of this town, calling upon its inhabitants to provide themselves with arms for their defense, was a measure as [[☃ definition 6]] as it was legal. . . . It is a natural right which the people have reserved to themselves, confirmed by the [English] Bill of Rights to keep arms for their own defense; and as Mr. Blackstone observes: it is to be made use of when the sanctions of society and law are found insufficient to restrain the violence of oppression.

**According to the excerpt, what is one reason that the Founding generation believed it was necessary for citizens to have the right to arm themselves?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: officers in charge of keeping the peace

choices.content: To prevent abuses by a tyrannical government
To protect one’s property from foreign invaders
To secure other individual rights like the right to vote
To ensure that all citizens know how to use  weapons

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Antonin Scalia’s majority opinion in *District of Columbia v. Heller* (2008).**

In any event, the meaning of “bear arms” that [[☃ definition 4]] and Justice Stevens propose is not even the (sometimes) [[☃ definition 1]] meaning. Rather, they manufacture a hybrid definition, whereby “bear arms” [[☃ definition 2]] the actual carrying of arms (and therefore is not really an idiom) but only in the service of an organized militia. No dictionary has ever adopted that definition, and we have been [[☃ definition 3]] of no source that indicates that it carried that meaning at the time of the founding. But it is easy to see why petitioners and the dissent are driven to the hybrid definition. Giving “bear Arms” its idiomatic meaning would cause the protected right to consist of the right to be a soldier or to wage war—an absurdity that no commentator has ever endorsed. 

**What is one reason Justice Scalia criticizes the position of the petitioners and the dissenting justices?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: relating to an idiom, or a group of words that has a special meaning
those who formally ask a court to hear a case

choices.content: It does not take into account how weaponry has changed since the founding.
It expands the right to bear arms beyond the vision of the founders.
It relies on an uncommon definition of “bear arms” that limits the right too much.
It is a historical definition that no longer applies to modern times.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Justice Antonin Scalia’s majority opinion in *District of Columbia v. Heller* (2008).**

In any event, the meaning of “bear arms” that [[☃ definition 2]] and Justice Stevens propose is not even the (sometimes) [[☃ definition 3]] meaning. Rather, they manufacture a hybrid definition, whereby “bear arms” [[☃ definition 4]] the actual carrying of arms (and therefore is not really an idiom) but only in the service of an organized militia. No dictionary has ever adopted that definition, and we have been [[☃ definition 5]] of no source that indicates that it carried that meaning at the time of the founding. But it is easy to see why petitioners and the dissent are driven to the hybrid definition. Giving “bear Arms” its idiomatic meaning would cause the protected right to consist of the right to be a soldier or to wage war—an absurdity that no commentator has ever endorsed. 

**This excerpt best serves as evidence of which statement about the significance of *District of Columbia v. Heller* (2008)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: those who formally ask a court to hear a case
relating to an idiom, or a group of words that has a special meaning

choices.content: The Court limited the Second Amendment to soldiers after taking into account historical context.
The Court ruled that the Second Amendment now applied to state and local governments.
The Court ruled for the first time that the Second Amendment applied to organized militias.
The Court defined the words of the Second Amendment for the first time in decades.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s majority opinion in *McDonald v. City of Chicago* (2012).**

[In a recent case], we held that the Second Amendment protects the right to keep and bear arms for the purpose of self-defense, and we struck down a District of Columbia law that banned the possession of handguns in the home. The city of Chicago (City) and the village of Oak Park, a Chicago suburb, have laws that are similar to the District of Columbia’s, but Chicago and Oak Park argue that their laws are constitutional because the Second Amendment has no application to the States. We have previously held that most of the provisions of the Bill of Rights apply with full force to both the Federal Government and the States. Applying the standard . . . we hold that the Second Amendment right is fully applicable to the States. . . .

**Based on the passage, which statement best describes the significance of the Supreme Court’s decision in *McDonald v. City of Chicago* (2012)?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It defined what limits the federal government can place on gun ownership.
It ruled that states must be held to a different standard than federal territories when applying Second Amendment protections.
It extended the right to bear arms to carrying arms outside the home.
It incorporated the Second Amendment to apply to state and local governments.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Court’s majority opinion in *McDonald v. City of Chicago* (2012).**

[In a recent case], we held that the Second Amendment protects the right to keep and bear arms for the purpose of self-defense, and we struck down a District of Columbia law that banned the possession of handguns in the home. The city of Chicago (City) and the village of Oak Park, a Chicago suburb, have laws that are similar to the District of Columbia’s, but Chicago and Oak Park argue that their laws are constitutional because the Second Amendment has no application to the States. We have previously held that most of the provisions of the Bill of Rights apply with full force to both the Federal Government and the States. Applying the standard . . . we hold that the Second Amendment right is fully applicable to the States. . . .

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: State governments can limit the right to bear arms only in public places, not in private homes.
State governments must follow any reasonable federal restrictions, like background checks, due to the Supremacy Clause.
State governments cannot infringe on an individual's right to own a firearm for the purpose of self-defense.
State governments do not have to allow citizens to own a firearm, as the Second Amendment applies only to the federal government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *New York State Rifle & Pistol Association v. Bruen* (2022).**

Today . . . we hold that when the Second Amendment’s plain text covers an individual’s conduct, the Constitution [[☃ definition 1]] protects that conduct. To justify its regulation, the government may not simply [[☃ definition 2]] that the regulation promotes an important interest. Rather, the government must demonstrate that the regulation is consistent with this Nation’s historical tradition of firearm regulation. Only if a firearm regulation is consistent with this Nation’s historical tradition may a court conclude that the individual’s conduct falls outside the Second Amendment’s “[[☃ definition 3]] command.” . . .

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: in a way that assumes something is true or correct
assume or suggest that something is true in order to make an argument

choices.content: All weapons covered under the Second Amendment must be weapons that were used during the Revolutionary era.
Any government regulation on the right to bear arms must be consistent with a regulation that existed in American history.
Any firearm regulation must relate to is a national interest or address a public safety concern for the regulation to be valid.
All people who own  weapons must keep them in their homes, as the Second Amendment historically applied only to militia members.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Virginia State Ratifying Convention’s Proposed Amendments (1788).**

That the people have a right to keep and bear arms: that a well regulated militia composed of the [[☃ definition 1]] of the people trained to arms, is the proper, natural and safe defense of a free State. That standing armies in time of peace are dangerous to liberty, and therefore ought to be avoided, as far as the circumstances and protection of the community will [[☃ definition 2]]; and that in all cases, the military should be under strict [[☃ definition 3]] to and governed by the civil power.

**This excerpt best serves as evidence for which popular belief in Founding-era America?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: being lower in rank and under the power of another

choices.content: Standing armies would threaten citizens’ freedom, and militias were a better way to defend the nation.
States should have sole responsibility for organizing and managing the American military.
The federal government should take on the important responsibility of training the nation’s standing army.
Militias should be disbanded after the American Revolution, as the government doesn’t have funds to pay for them.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Virginia State Ratifying Convention’s Proposed Amendments (1788).**

That the people have a right to keep and bear arms: that a well regulated militia composed of the [[☃ definition 1]] of the people trained to arms, is the proper, natural and safe defense of a free State. That standing armies in time of peace are dangerous to liberty, and therefore ought to be avoided, as far as the circumstances and protection of the community will [[☃ definition 2]]; and that in all cases, the military should be under strict [[☃ definition 3]] to and governed by the civil power.

**Based on the passage, which statement best describes the Virginia State Ratifying Convention’s view on standing armies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: being lower in rank and under the power of another

choices.content: They are necessary for the national defense and should be under federal control.
They should be responsible for everyday policing and the protection of local communities.
They should exist only when the country is at war, as they can be used to oppress the people.
They must be staffed by members of local militias, as militia members are well trained for combat.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 28 by Alexander Hamilton.**

If the representatives of the people betray their [[☃ definition 1]], there is then no [[☃ definition 5]] left but in the [[☃ definition 2]] of that original right of self-defense which is [[☃ definition 3]] to all positive forms of government, and which against the [[☃ definition 4]] of the national rulers may be exerted with infinitely better prospect of success than against those of the rulers of an individual State. 

**Which statement best summarizes Hamilton’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the people who live in the district that an elected representative represents

choices.content: An individual’s right to bear arms is necessary in order to protect citizens against tyrannical governments.
Protecting an individual’s right to bear arms is dangerous because it encourages people to engage in violence against the government.
The Constitution gives people nonviolent methods to dissolve tyrannical governments, making the right to bear arms unnecessary.
Only people who live under a monarchy have a right to bear arms, as they cannot vote their representatives out like the U.S. citizens can.

assessment_items.question_content:
*This is an excerpt from “Remarks on the First Part of the Amendments to the Federal Constitution” (1789) by Trench Coxe. Coxe is writing in response to the proposed Bill of Rights.**

As civil rulers, not having their duty to the people before them, may attempt to [[☃ definition 2]], and as the military forces which must be occasionally raised to defend our country, might [[☃ definition 1]] their power to the injury of their fellow citizens, the people are confirmed by the [second] article in their right to keep and bear their private arms.

**Which statement best summarizes Coxe’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: rule over others through force or cruelty

choices.content: The Constitution affirms the right to bear arms, but only for members of the military during wartime.
The Second Amendment protects citizens’ right to bear arms, which is necessary to resist tyranny and abuse of power.
The risk of tyranny  in a republic is so small that there is no need for the protections of the proposed Second Amendment.
The creation of a national military means that there is no need for state militias composed of privately armed citizens.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Representative Sidney Clarke’s comments during the debates over the Civil Rights Act of 1866, the first national civil rights law in the U.S. **

Who, sir, were those men? Not the present militia; but the brave black soldiers of the Union, disarmed and robbed by this wicked and [[☃ definition 1]] order. Nearly every white man in [Mississippi] that could bear arms was in the rebel ranks. Nearly all of their able-bodied colored men who could reach our lines enlisted under the old flag. Many of these brave defenders of the nation paid for their arms with which they went to battle. And I regret, sir, that justice compels me to say, to the disgrace of the Federal Government, that the "reconstructed" state authorities of Mississippi were allowed to rob and disarm our veteran soldiers and arm the rebels fresh from the field of [[☃ definition 2]] [[☃ definition 3]]. Sir, the disarmed loyalists of Alabama, Mississippi, and Louisiana are powerless today, and oppressed by the pardoned and encouraged rebels of those States.

**Representative Clarke criticizes which development after the Civil War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: relating to rulers who have total power and use it in a cruel way

choices.content: The continued enslavement of Black people despite federal legislation
The removal of Black soldiers from the military despite their previous service
The rise of militias that aimed to force free Black people to relocate from the South
The unfair restriction of the right of Black Americans to bear arms
